---
title: "Session 9: Extensions, Reference & Visualizations"
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: left
    code-fold: false
editor: visual
---

## Overview

This session is a technical reference for the extended R modeling ecosystem. The focus is on syntax, packages, and practical workflow. Each section introduces the relevant packages, shows the key function calls, and demonstrates the output. Treat this document as a reference you can return to when a specific model type or visualization comes up in your own work.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Setup

```{r, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# GLM extensions
library(MASS)          # polr(), glm.nb()
library(nnet)          # multinom()
library(VGAM)          # vglm(), wide family support
library(geepack)       # geeglm()
library(ordinal)       # clm(), cumulative link models
library(betareg)       # beta regression for proportions

# Model fit & testing
library(lmtest)        # lrtest(), waldtest(), coeftest()
library(car)           # Anova() Type II/III
library(performance)   # check_model(), compare_performance()
library(DescTools)     # PseudoR2()

# Classification
library(pROC)          # roc(), auc(), ggroc()

# Survival
library(survival)      # Surv(), survfit(), coxph()
library(ggsurvfit)     # ggplot2-native KM plots
library(survminer)     # ggcoxzph(), ggforest()

# Forest plots & tables
library(finalfit)      # finalfit(), or_plot(), hr_plot()
library(forestplot)    # manual forest plots
library(gtsummary)     # tbl_regression(), tbl_summary()

# Propensity scores
library(MatchIt)       # matchit()
library(WeightIt)      # weightit() for IPW
library(cobalt)        # love.plot(), bal.tab()

# Visualization
library(factoextra)    # fviz_pca_biplot(), fviz_eig()
library(ggcorrplot)    # ggcorrplot()
library(pheatmap)      # pheatmap()
library(ggridges)      # geom_density_ridges()
library(ggrepel)       # geom_text_repel() for volcano plots

# Time series
library(forecast)      # auto.arima(), forecast(), autoplot()

# Core
library(tidyverse)
library(broom)
library(broom.mixed)
library(patchwork)
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Part 1: GLM Extensions & Package Ecosystem

## What Base `glm()` Supports

Base R's `glm()` covers the core exponential family distributions. Everything in the table below works out of the box with no additional packages:

| Family | Link options | Typical use case |
|-----------------|------------------------|-------------------------------|
| `gaussian()` | `identity`, `log`, `inverse` | Continuous response (= `lm`) |
| `binomial()` | `logit`, `probit`, `cloglog` | Binary 0/1, proportions |
| `poisson()` | `log`, `identity`, `sqrt` | Counts, rates |
| `Gamma()` | `inverse`, `log`, `identity` | Positive continuous, skewed |
| `inverse.gaussian()` | `1/mu^2`, `inverse`, `log` | Positive, heavy right tail |
| `quasi()` | any | Variance function without full distribution assumption |
| `quasibinomial()` | `logit` | Binomial with overdispersion |
| `quasipoisson()` | `log` | Poisson with overdispersion |

**What base `glm()` cannot do:** ordinal or multinomial responses, zero-inflated counts, negative binomial with estimated dispersion, correlated/clustered outcomes (GEE or GLMM), beta regression for proportions strictly between 0 and 1, or robust variance estimation.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Extended GLM Package Reference Table

This table covers the most commonly used packages for models that go beyond base `glm()`. Packages are grouped by response type.

### Binary, Ordinal & Multinomial Responses

| Package | Model | Key function | Notes |
|----------------|----------------|------------------------|----------------|
| Base R | Logistic (logit) | `glm(..., family=binomial)` | Default; logit link |
| Base R | Probit | `glm(..., family=binomial(link="probit"))` | Same syntax, different link |
| Base R | Complementary log-log | `glm(..., family=binomial(link="cloglog"))` | Asymmetric; grouped survival data |
| `MASS` | Ordinal (proportional odds) | `polr(y ~ x, data, Hess=TRUE)` | Classic PO model; `tidy()` works |
| `ordinal` | Ordinal (cumulative link) | `clm(y ~ x, data, link="logit")` | More flexible than `polr`; mixed effects via `clmm()` |
| `nnet` | Multinomial logistic | `multinom(y ~ x, data)` | Baseline-category logit; fast |
| `VGAM` | Multinomial, ordinal, many more | `vglm(y ~ x, family=multinomial)` | Huge family library; see Part 1.5 |

### Count & Rate Responses

| Package | Model | Key function | Notes |
|----------------|----------------|------------------------|----------------|
| Base R | Poisson | `glm(..., family=poisson)` | Assumes mean = variance |
| Base R | Quasi-Poisson | `glm(..., family=quasipoisson)` | Overdispersion correction; no likelihood |
| `MASS` | Negative binomial | `glm.nb(y ~ x, data)` | Estimates dispersion parameter |


### Correlated & Clustered Outcomes

| Package | Model | Key function | Notes |
|----------------|----------------|------------------------|----------------|
| `geepack` | GEE (population-average) | `geeglm(y ~ x, id=id, corstr="exchangeable")` | Marginal interpretation; robust SE |
| `lme4` | GLMM (subject-specific) | `glmer(y ~ x + (1|id), family=binomial)` | Conditional interpretation |
| `ordinal` | Ordinal GLMM | `clmm(y ~ x + (1|id), data)` | Mixed ordinal model |

### Specialized Responses

| Package | Model | Key function | Notes |
|----------------|----------------|------------------------|----------------|
| `betareg` | Beta regression (proportions) | `betareg(y ~ x, data)` | Response strictly in (0,1) |
| `VGAM` | Tobit, log-normal, many others | `vglm(y ~ x, family=tobit)` | See VGAM section |

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Probit & Link Function Comparison

All three binary link functions use identical `glm()` syntax — only the `link` argument changes.

```{r}
data(Default, package = "ISLR2")

mod_logit  <- glm(default ~ balance + income, data = Default, family = binomial(link = "logit"))
mod_probit <- glm(default ~ balance + income, data = Default, family = binomial(link = "probit"))
mod_cloglog <- glm(default ~ balance + income, data = Default, family = binomial(link = "cloglog"))
```

The coefficients are on different scales but the predicted probabilities will be similar in the middle of the distribution and differ more in the tails. The cloglog link is asymmetric — it approaches 0 more slowly than 1, making it appropriate when the event is rare or the data have a grouped survival structure.

```{r, fig.width=9, fig.height=4}
# Compare predicted probabilities across a balance grid
grid <- tibble(
  balance = seq(0, 2700, length.out = 300),
  income  = median(Default$income)
) %>%
  mutate(
    logit   = predict(mod_logit,   newdata = ., type = "response"),
    probit  = predict(mod_probit,  newdata = ., type = "response"),
    cloglog = predict(mod_cloglog, newdata = ., type = "response")
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(logit:cloglog, names_to = "link", values_to = "prob")

ggplot(grid, aes(x = balance, y = prob, color = link)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("logit" = "steelblue", "probit" = "tomato", "cloglog" = "forestgreen")) +
  labs(title = "Predicted P(Default) by Link Function",
       subtitle = "Income held at median",
       x = "Balance", y = "Predicted probability", color = "Link") +
  theme_minimal()
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Ordinal Logistic Regression

Use when the outcome has ordered categories (e.g., pain severity: none / mild / moderate / severe) and you want to model the cumulative probability of being at or below each level.

### `MASS::polr()` — proportional odds model

```{r}
data(housing, package = "MASS")   # housing satisfaction data

mod_polr <- polr(Sat ~ Infl + Type + Cont, data = housing,
                 weights = Freq, Hess = TRUE)
summary(mod_polr)
```

```{r}
# tidy() gives coefficients + intercepts (the cutpoints between categories)
tidy(mod_polr, conf.int = TRUE, exponentiate = TRUE) %>%
  print(n = 20)
```

Coefficients exponentiated are **odds ratios** for moving up one level in the ordinal scale. The `|` rows (e.g., `Low|Medium`) are the cutpoint intercepts — they define where the cumulative probability curve crosses each threshold.

### `ordinal::clm()` — cumulative link model

`clm()` fits the same model but offers more link functions (`logit`, `probit`, `cloglog`, `cauchit`) and a cleaner interface for testing the proportional odds assumption via `nominal_test()`.

```{r}
mod_clm <- clm(Sat ~ Infl + Type + Cont, data = housing, weights = Freq)
summary(mod_clm)
```

```{r}
# Test proportional odds assumption — significant result = assumption violated
nominal_test(mod_clm)
```

A significant result here means a predictor's effect is not constant across thresholds, violating the proportional odds assumption. In that case, consider a partial proportional odds model or multinomial logistic regression.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Multinomial Logistic Regression

Use when the outcome has unordered categories (e.g., transportation mode: car / bus / bike).

### `nnet::multinom()`

```{r, message=FALSE}
mod_multi <- multinom(Species ~ Sepal.Length + Sepal.Width + Petal.Length,
                      data = iris, trace = FALSE)
summary(mod_multi)
```

```{r}
tidy(mod_multi, exponentiate = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE)
```

Each row is one predictor for one non-reference category vs the baseline. Exponentiated coefficients are **relative risk ratios** (RRR) — the ratio of probabilities of being in that category vs the baseline for a one-unit change in the predictor. Reference category is the first level of the response factor.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## VGAM: Vector Generalized Additive Models

`VGAM` is a large package with its own modeling engine — `vglm()` — that supports a very wide range of response distributions and link combinations not available elsewhere. The syntax is similar to `glm()` but uses `vglm()` and VGAM-specific family objects.

```{r, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
library(VGAM)
```

### Key families in VGAM

| Family | Use case |
|----|----|
| `multinomial()` | Multinomial logistic regression |
| `cumulative(parallel=TRUE)` | Proportional odds (ordinal) |
| `cumulative(parallel=FALSE)` | Non-proportional odds (unconstrained) |
| `acat()` | Adjacent categories logit model |
| `cratio()` | Continuation ratio model |
| `tobit()` | Tobit regression for censored continuous outcomes |
| `posnegbinomial()` | Positive negative binomial (zero-truncated) |
| `beta.ab()` | Beta regression |
| `zipoissonff()` | Zero-inflated Poisson |

### Example: multinomial with `vglm()`

```{r}
mod_vglm <- vglm(Species ~ Sepal.Length + Sepal.Width + Petal.Length,
                 family = multinomial,
                 data   = iris)
summary(mod_vglm)
```

```{r}
coef(mod_vglm, matrix = TRUE)   # coefficients organised by response category
```

### Example: proportional odds with `vglm()`

```{r}
# Ordered factor needed
iris_ord <- iris %>%
  mutate(Sepal.cut = cut(Sepal.Width, breaks = 3,
                         labels = c("narrow", "medium", "wide"),
                         ordered = TRUE))

mod_po <- vglm(Sepal.cut ~ Petal.Length + Petal.Width,
               family = cumulative(parallel = TRUE, link = "logitlink"),
               data   = iris_ord)
summary(mod_po)
```

Setting `parallel = FALSE` relaxes the proportional odds assumption and estimates separate slopes for each threshold — a direct test of whether PO holds.

### GEE Syntax Reference — `geepack`

GEE models the population-average (marginal) effect. Coefficients are interpreted the same way as the equivalent GLM, but standard errors are robust to within-cluster correlation. The key extra arguments are `id` (cluster identifier) and `corstr` (working correlation structure).

```{r, eval=FALSE}
library(geepack)

# Working correlation structures: "independence", "exchangeable", "ar1", "unstructured"
mod_gee <- geeglm(
  y ~ x1 + x2,
  data    = df,
  id      = subject_id,          # cluster variable (unquoted or as variable)
  family  = binomial,
  corstr  = "exchangeable",      # assumed correlation structure within clusters
  std.err = "san.se"             # sandwich (robust) SE — default
)

summary(mod_gee)
tidy(mod_gee, exponentiate = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE)
```

**Choosing `corstr`:**

| Structure | When to use |
|----------------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| `"independence"` | Ignore clustering (wrong but sometimes compared against) |
| `"exchangeable"` | All observations within cluster equally correlated (most common) |
| `"ar1"` | Correlation decays with time lag — longitudinal data |
| `"unstructured"` | Estimate all pairwise correlations — needs many clusters |

GEE vs GLMM: GEE gives you the population-average effect ("how does the average person change?"); GLMM gives you the subject-specific effect ("how does a particular person change?"). For binary outcomes the two estimates are numerically different even in expectation.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Part 2: Deviance, Testing & Model Fit Reference

## Testing Reference Table

Different packages and model types require different testing approaches. This table covers the main options:

| Test | Function | Package | When to use |
|-----------------|------------------|-----------------|----------------------|
| Likelihood ratio test | `anova(mod1, mod2, test="Chisq")` | Base R | Nested GLMs; most common |
| LRT (explicit) | `lrtest(mod1, mod2)` | `lmtest` | Same as above, cleaner output |
| Wald test | `waldtest(mod1, mod2)` | `lmtest` | Faster approximation; less accurate in small samples |
| Wald test (coef) | `coeftest(mod, vcov=sandwich)` | `lmtest` + `sandwich` | Robust SE-based inference |
| Type II/III ANOVA | `Anova(mod, type="II")` | `car` | Handles unbalanced designs; works on `glm`, `lm`, `lmer` |
| Score test | `anova(mod, test="Rao")` | Base R | Alternative to LRT; useful when full model is hard to fit |
| Deviance goodness-of-fit | `1 - pchisq(deviance(mod), df.residual(mod))` | Base R | For Poisson: tests if model fits better than saturated |
| Overdispersion test | `dispersiontest(mod)` | `AER` | Formal test of Poisson equidispersion |
| PH assumption (Cox) | `cox.zph(mod)` | `survival` | Tests proportional hazards via Schoenfeld residuals |
| PO assumption (ordinal) | `nominal_test(mod)` | `ordinal` | Tests proportional odds via score test |

## Goodness-of-Fit Metrics Reference Table

| Metric | Function | Package | Notes |
|-----------------|--------------------|------------------|-----------------|
| AIC | `AIC(mod)` / `glance(mod)$AIC` | Base R / `broom` | Lower = better; penalizes parameters |
| BIC | `BIC(mod)` / `glance(mod)$BIC` | Base R / `broom` | Stronger penalty than AIC; favors parsimony |
| McFadden's R² | `PseudoR2(mod, "McFadden")` | `DescTools` | 0.2–0.4 considered good; most common pseudo-R² |
| Nagelkerke R² | `PseudoR2(mod, "Nagelkerke")` | `DescTools` | Scaled 0–1; easier to communicate |
| Cox-Snell R² | `PseudoR2(mod, "CoxSnell")` | `DescTools` | Cannot reach 1 for binary outcomes |
| Tjur's R² | `PseudoR2(mod, "Tjur")` | `DescTools` | Discrimination-based; binary only |
| All pseudo-R² | `PseudoR2(mod)` | `DescTools` | Returns all available metrics at once |
| R² (various) | `r2(mod)` | `performance` | Smart dispatch; works on `lm`, `glm`, `lmer` |
| Model comparison | `compare_performance(m1, m2, m3)` | `performance` | Side-by-side table with multiple metrics |
[Performance package details](https://easystats.github.io/performance/)

![Performance Workflow](https://easystats.github.io/performance/reference/figures/figure_workflow.png)

### Multi-model comparison pipeline

```{r, eval=FALSE}
# Fit several candidate models
models <- list(
  m1 = glm(y ~ x1,           data = df, family = binomial),
  m2 = glm(y ~ x1 + x2,      data = df, family = binomial),
  m3 = glm(y ~ x1 + x2 + x3, data = df, family = binomial)
)

# AIC/BIC table with broom
models %>%
  map(glance) %>%
  bind_rows(.id = "model") %>%
  select(model, AIC, BIC, deviance, df.residual, nobs)

# Or with performance
library(performance)
compare_performance(models$m1, models$m2, models$m3, rank = TRUE)
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Part 3: ROC Curves & Classification Diagnostics

## `pROC` Core Workflow

```{r, message=FALSE}
library(pROC)

data(Default, package = "ISLR2")
mod_logit <- glm(default ~ balance + income + student,
                 data = Default, family = binomial)

# Build the ROC object
roc_obj <- roc(
  response  = Default$default,
  predictor = predict(mod_logit, type = "response"),
  levels    = c("No", "Yes"),   # negative level first
  quiet     = TRUE
)

roc_obj        # prints AUC
auc(roc_obj)   # extract AUC
ci.auc(roc_obj)  # 95% CI via DeLong method
```

### ggplot-native ROC curve

`ggroc()` returns a `ggplot` object so all standard `ggplot2` layers apply:

```{r}
ggroc(roc_obj, color = "steelblue", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +
  annotate("text", x = 0.4, y = 0.2,
           label = paste0("AUC = ", round(auc(roc_obj), 3)),
           size = 4.5, color = "steelblue") +
  labs(title = "ROC Curve — Default Model",
       x = "Specificity", y = "Sensitivity") +
  theme_minimal()
```

Note: `ggroc()` plots specificity on the x-axis running right to left (high specificity on the left) — this matches the traditional ROC orientation.

### Comparing two models on one plot

```{r}
mod_simple <- glm(default ~ balance, data = Default, family = binomial)

roc_simple <- roc(Default$default,
                  predict(mod_simple, type = "response"),
                  levels = c("No", "Yes"), quiet = TRUE)

roc_full   <- roc(Default$default,
                  predict(mod_logit, type = "response"),
                  levels = c("No", "Yes"), quiet = TRUE)

ggroc(list("Balance only" = roc_simple, "Full model" = roc_full),
      linewidth = 1) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Balance only" = "tomato", "Full model" = "steelblue")) +
  labs(title = "ROC Curve Comparison", x = "Specificity",
       y = "Sensitivity", color = "Model") +
  theme_minimal()
```

```{r}
# Formal DeLong test comparing two ROC curves
roc.test(roc_simple, roc_full)
```

### Threshold selection with `coords()`

`coords()` returns sensitivity and specificity at every threshold. The Youden index (sensitivity + specificity − 1) is maximized at the optimal operating point.

```{r}
# All thresholds with sens/spec
all_coords <- coords(roc_full, x = "all", ret = c("threshold", "sensitivity", "specificity"))

# Optimal threshold by Youden index
best <- coords(roc_full, x = "best", best.method = "youden",
               ret = c("threshold", "sensitivity", "specificity"))
best
```

```{r}
# Annotate optimal point on ROC plot
ggroc(roc_full, color = "steelblue", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +
  geom_point(aes(x = best$specificity, y = best$sensitivity),
             color = "tomato", size = 4) +
  annotate("text", x = best$specificity - 0.08, y = best$sensitivity - 0.04,
           label = paste0("Threshold = ", round(best$threshold, 3)),
           color = "tomato", size = 3.5) +
  labs(title = "ROC with Optimal Threshold (Youden Index)",
       x = "Specificity", y = "Sensitivity") +
  theme_minimal()
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Part 4: Survival Analysis

## Survival Objects & Kaplan-Meier

The `survival` package is the foundation. Every survival analysis starts with a `Surv()` object that pairs event times with event/censoring status.

```{r}
data(lung, package = "survival")
glimpse(lung)
```

`lung` contains survival data for 228 patients with advanced lung cancer. Key variables: `time` (days), `status` (1=censored, 2=dead), `sex` (1=male, 2=female), `age`, `ph.ecog` (ECOG performance score).

```{r}
# Recode status to 0/1 (0=censored, 1=event) — tidier for most functions
lung <- lung %>%
  mutate(
    status = ifelse(status == 2, 1, 0),
    sex    = factor(sex, levels = c(1, 2), labels = c("Male", "Female"))
  )
```

### `survfit()` and Kaplan-Meier estimates

```{r}
# Overall KM curve
km_overall <- survfit(Surv(time, status) ~ 1, data = lung)
summary(km_overall, times = c(180, 365, 540))
```

```{r}
# Stratified by sex
km_sex <- survfit(Surv(time, status) ~ sex, data = lung)
km_sex
```

### KM plots with `ggsurvfit`

`ggsurvfit` gives `ggplot2`-native KM plots. The `add_risktable()` layer adds the number at risk below the plot — standard in clinical publications.

```{r, fig.width=8, fig.height=6}
km_sex %>%
  ggsurvfit(linewidth = 1) +
  add_confidence_interval(alpha = 0.15) +
  add_risktable(risktable_stats = "n.risk") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Male" = "steelblue", "Female" = "tomato")) +
  scale_fill_manual(values  = c("Male" = "steelblue", "Female" = "tomato")) +
  labs(title = "Kaplan-Meier Survival Curves by Sex",
       subtitle = "Lung cancer dataset (survival package)",
       x = "Days", y = "Survival probability") +
  theme_minimal()
```

### Log-rank test

```{r}
survdiff(Surv(time, status) ~ sex, data = lung)
```

The log-rank test compares survival curves across groups under the null that they are identical. The chi-squared statistic and p-value are printed directly.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Cox Proportional Hazards

```{r}
mod_cox <- coxph(Surv(time, status) ~ sex + age + ph.ecog,
                 data = lung)
summary(mod_cox)
```

```{r}
# Tidy output — exponentiate for hazard ratios
tidy(mod_cox, exponentiate = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE)
```

```{r}
glance(mod_cox)
```

Hazard ratios \> 1 indicate increased hazard (shorter survival); \< 1 indicate decreased hazard. Here a HR for `sex` (Female vs Male) \< 1 means women have lower hazard of death.

### Second dataset: `veteran`

```{r}
data(veteran, package = "survival")

mod_cox2 <- coxph(Surv(time, status) ~ trt + celltype + karno + diagtime + age + prior,
                  data = veteran)
tidy(mod_cox2, exponentiate = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE)
```

### Testing the proportional hazards assumption

The PH assumption requires that the hazard ratio between any two individuals is constant over time. `cox.zph()` tests this via Schoenfeld residuals — a significant p-value for a covariate means its effect is not constant over time.

```{r}
ph_test <- cox.zph(mod_cox)
ph_test
```

```{r, fig.width=9, fig.height=6}
# Plot Schoenfeld residuals — should be flat if PH holds
ggcoxzph(ph_test)
```

A non-flat smoothed line in the Schoenfeld residual plot indicates time-varying effects. Common remedies: stratify by the violating variable (`strata()` in the formula), add a time interaction (`tt()` in `coxph`), or use a time-varying coefficient model.


------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Part 5: Forest Plots & 'Publication' Tables

## `finalfit` — Lead Workflow

`finalfit` is designed for clinical data analysis and produces publication-ready tables and plots directly from model objects. It integrates variable labels, handles missing data, and generates forest plots in a single call.

```{r, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
library(finalfit)
```

### `summary_factorlist()` — Table 1

```{r}
lung %>%
  summary_factorlist(
    dependent   = "status",
    explanatory = c("age", "sex", "ph.ecog"),
    p           = TRUE,
    add_dependent_label = TRUE
  )
```

### `finalfit()` — regression table with confidence intervals

```{r}
lung %>%
  finalfit(
    dependent   = "status",
    explanatory = c("sex", "age", "ph.ecog")
  )
```

The output combines univariate and multivariable results in one table — the format expected in most clinical journals.

### `or_plot()` — logistic regression forest plot

```{r, fig.width=8, fig.height=4, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
Default %>%
  mutate(default = as.numeric(default == "Yes")) %>%
  or_plot(
    dependent   = "default",
    explanatory = c("balance", "income", "student")
  )
```

### `hr_plot()` — Cox regression forest plot

```{r, fig.width=8, fig.height=4, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
lung %>%
  hr_plot(
    dependent   = "Surv(time, status)",
    explanatory = c("sex", "age", "ph.ecog")
  )
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## `forestplot` Package — Manual Forest Plots

`forestplot` gives you complete control over the appearance. It's built around a data frame of labels, means, and confidence intervals — useful when `finalfit` doesn't quite produce the layout you need.

```{r, message=FALSE}
library(forestplot)

# Extract HR estimates from Cox model
cox_tidy <- tidy(mod_cox, exponentiate = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE) %>%
  filter(term != "(Intercept)")

# Build the table structure forestplot expects
fp_data <- tibble(
  label     = cox_tidy$term,
  mean      = cox_tidy$estimate,
  lower     = cox_tidy$conf.low,
  upper     = cox_tidy$conf.high
)

fp_data %>%
  forestplot(
    labeltext = label,
    mean      = mean,
    lower     = lower,
    upper     = upper,
    zero      = 1,                        # reference line at HR=1
    boxsize   = 0.2,
    col       = fpColors(box = "steelblue", line = "steelblue", zero = "grey50"),
    xlab      = "Hazard Ratio (95% CI)",
    title     = "Cox Model: Lung Cancer"
  )
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## `gtsummary` — Publication Tables

```{r, message=FALSE}
library(gtsummary)
```

### Descriptive table (`tbl_summary`)

```{r}
lung %>%
  select(age, sex, ph.ecog, status) %>%
  tbl_summary(
    by      = sex,
    missing = "no"
  ) %>%
  add_p() %>%
  add_overall()
```

### Regression table (`tbl_regression`)

```{r}
mod_cox %>%
  tbl_regression(
    exponentiate = TRUE,
    label        = list(sex ~ "Sex", age ~ "Age (years)", ph.ecog ~ "ECOG score")
  ) %>%
  bold_p() %>%
  add_glance_source_note()
```


------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Part 6: Propensity Score Methods

## Setup and Data

```{r}
data(lalonde, package = "MatchIt")
glimpse(lalonde)
```

`lalonde` is a classic dataset studying the effect of a job training program (`treat`) on 1978 earnings (`re78`). The treatment is not randomly assigned, so observed covariates need to be balanced.

```{r}
# Covariate balance before matching
lalonde %>%
  group_by(treat) %>%
  summarise(across(c(age, educ, re74, re75), mean)) %>%
  pivot_longer(-treat) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = treat, values_from = value, names_prefix = "treat_") %>%
  mutate(diff = treat_1 - treat_0)
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Matching with `MatchIt`

`matchit()` estimates propensity scores and performs matching in one call. The most common approach is 1:1 nearest-neighbor matching on the logit of the propensity score.

```{r}
library(MatchIt)

m_out <- matchit(
  treat ~ age + educ + race + married + nodegree + re74 + re75,
  data   = lalonde,
  method = "nearest",    # nearest-neighbor matching
  ratio  = 1,            # 1:1 matching
  distance = "logit"     # match on logit of PS
)

summary(m_out, un = FALSE)   # balance after matching
```

### Balance visualization with `cobalt`

```{r, fig.width=7, fig.height=5, message=FALSE}
library(cobalt)

love.plot(
  m_out,
  thresholds  = c(m = 0.1),   # 0.1 SMD threshold line
  colors      = c("tomato", "steelblue"),
  shapes      = c("circle", "circle"),
  sample.names = c("Unmatched", "Matched"),
  title = "Covariate Balance: Before and After Matching"
)
```

Points to the left of the dashed line (SMD \< 0.1) are considered well-balanced. The goal is to bring all covariates inside this threshold.

### Outcome analysis on matched data

```{r}
# Extract matched dataset
matched_data <- match.data(m_out)

# Outcome model on matched sample — include matching weights
mod_matched <- lm(re78 ~ treat + age + educ + re74 + re75,
                  data    = matched_data,
                  weights = weights)

tidy(mod_matched, conf.int = TRUE) %>%
  filter(term == "treat")
```

The coefficient on `treat` is the estimated average treatment effect on the treated (ATT) after covariate adjustment.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Inverse Probability Weighting with `WeightIt`

IPW keeps the full dataset and reweights observations to create a pseudo-population where treatment is independent of measured confounders. It is more efficient than matching when the sample is large.

```{r, message=FALSE}
library(WeightIt)

w_out <- weightit(
  treat ~ age + educ + race + married + nodegree + re74 + re75,
  data   = lalonde,
  method = "ps",          # propensity score weighting
  estimand = "ATE"        # average treatment effect; use "ATT" for treated only
)

summary(w_out)
```

### Balance after IPW

```{r, fig.width=7, fig.height=5}
love.plot(
  w_out,
  thresholds   = c(m = 0.1),
  colors       = c("tomato", "steelblue"),
  sample.names = c("Unweighted", "IPW"),
  title = "Covariate Balance: IPW"
)
```

### Weighted outcome model

```{r}
mod_ipw <- lm(re78 ~ treat + age + educ + re74 + re75,
              data    = lalonde,
              weights = w_out$weights)

tidy(mod_ipw, conf.int = TRUE) %>%
  filter(term == "treat")
```

For more robust inference with IPW, use a weighted estimating equation via `survey::svyglm()` or the `sandwich` package to get HC-robust standard errors.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Part 7: Exploratory Visualizations

## `factoextra` — PCA Biplots

`factoextra` wraps base R's `prcomp()` output in `ggplot2`-compatible plots. The biplot shows both observations and variable loadings in the same space — a useful first look at multivariate structure.

```{r, fig.width=7, fig.height=6, message=FALSE}
library(factoextra)

pca_res <- prcomp(iris[, 1:4], scale. = TRUE)

fviz_pca_biplot(
  pca_res,
  geom.ind  = "point",
  col.ind   = iris$Species,
  palette   = c("steelblue", "tomato", "forestgreen"),
  addEllipses = TRUE,
  col.var   = "black",
  repel     = TRUE,
  legend.title = "Species",
  title = "PCA Biplot — Iris"
)
```

```{r, fig.width=6, fig.height=4}
fviz_eig(pca_res, addlabels = TRUE, barfill = "steelblue", barcolor = "steelblue") +
  labs(title = "Scree Plot — Variance Explained by Component") +
  theme_minimal()
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Correlation Heatmaps

```{r, fig.width=6, fig.height=5}
library(ggcorrplot)

corr_mat <- cor(mtcars, use = "complete.obs")

ggcorrplot(
  corr_mat,
  method   = "square",
  type     = "lower",
  lab      = TRUE,
  lab_size = 2.5,
  colors   = c("tomato", "white", "steelblue"),
  title    = "Correlation Matrix — mtcars"
)
```

```{r, fig.width=7, fig.height=6, message=FALSE}
library(pheatmap)

# Annotated heatmap — scale rows so patterns are visible
pheatmap(
  corr_mat,
  color            = colorRampPalette(c("tomato", "white", "steelblue"))(100),
  display_numbers  = TRUE,
  number_format    = "%.2f",
  fontsize_number  = 7,
  main             = "Correlation Heatmap — mtcars"
)
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Ridge Plots — Distribution Comparisons

`ggridges` is excellent for comparing distributions across many groups without overcrowding a standard density plot.

```{r, fig.width=7, fig.height=5, message=FALSE}
library(ggridges)

ggplot(diamonds %>% sample_n(5000),
       aes(x = price, y = cut, fill = cut)) +
  geom_density_ridges(alpha = 0.7, scale = 1.2, rel_min_height = 0.01) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Blues") +
  scale_x_log10(labels = scales::dollar) +
  labs(title = "Diamond Price Distribution by Cut",
       x = "Price (log scale)", y = NULL) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Part 8: Time Series — Simple Introduction

## Creating a `ts` Object and STL Decomposition

`ts()` creates a time series object from a numeric vector. The key arguments are `frequency` (observations per period: 12 for monthly, 4 for quarterly, 52 for weekly) and `start` (the first time point as a vector `c(year, period)`).

```{r}
# AirPassengers is a built-in monthly dataset: 1949-1960
data(AirPassengers)
class(AirPassengers)
AirPassengers
```

```{r, fig.width=8, fig.height=3}
autoplot(AirPassengers) +
  labs(title = "Monthly Airline Passengers 1949–1960",
       x = NULL, y = "Passengers (thousands)") +
  theme_minimal()
```

### STL decomposition

`stl()` (Seasonal and Trend decomposition using Loess) splits a time series into trend, seasonal, and remainder components. It handles changing seasonality better than classical decomposition.

```{r, fig.width=8, fig.height=6}
# Log-transform first to stabilize variance (multiplicative → additive)
ap_log <- log(AirPassengers)

stl_fit <- stl(ap_log, s.window = "periodic")

autoplot(stl_fit) +
  labs(title = "STL Decomposition — log(AirPassengers)") +
  theme_minimal()
```

The four panels show: observed series, seasonal component, trend component, and remainder. A strong seasonal pattern shows up clearly; the trend panel reveals steady growth across the decade.

```{r}
# Seasonal strength and trend strength (0=none, 1=strong)
var(remainder(stl_fit)) / var(seasadj(stl_fit))   # 1 - this = seasonal strength
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## `forecast` Package — Simple Forecasting

```{r, message=FALSE}
library(forecast)
```

### `auto.arima()` — automatic model selection

`auto.arima()` searches over ARIMA(p,d,q) model orders and selects the best by AIC. It handles differencing automatically.

```{r}
fit_arima <- auto.arima(AirPassengers, lambda = "auto")  # lambda="auto" for Box-Cox transform
summary(fit_arima)
```

### Forecasting and plotting

```{r, fig.width=9, fig.height=5}
fc <- forecast(fit_arima, h = 24)   # forecast 24 months ahead

autoplot(fc) +
  labs(title = "24-Month Forecast — Airline Passengers",
       subtitle = "ARIMA with automatic Box-Cox transform",
       x = NULL, y = "Passengers") +
  theme_minimal()
```

The dark shaded band is the 80% prediction interval; the light band is the 95% interval.

### Where to go next

For a modern tidyverse-native time series workflow, the `tsibble` / `fable` ecosystem (by the same authors as `tidyverse`) is the successor to `forecast`. Key packages:

| Package      | Role                                         |
|--------------|----------------------------------------------|
| `tsibble`    | Tidy time series data structure              |
| `feasts`     | Feature extraction and visualization         |
| `fable`      | Modelling and forecasting (ARIMA, ETS, etc.) |
| `fabletools` | Accuracy metrics, reconciliation             |

## Part 9: Exporting Tables & Figures

### Tables — `gtsummary`

`gtsummary` objects can be exported to multiple formats depending on your output target.

```{r, eval=FALSE}
tbl <- mod_cox %>%
  tbl_regression(exponentiate = TRUE)

# To Word (.docx) via flextable
tbl %>%
  as_flex_table() %>%
  flextable::save_as_docx(path = "cox_table.docx")

# To HTML (renders in Quarto/RMarkdown output)
tbl %>%
  as_kable_extra(format = "html")

# To LaTeX
tbl %>%
  as_kable_extra(format = "latex", booktabs = TRUE)
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Tables — `knitr::kable()` and `kableExtra`

For plain tibbles and data frames, `kable()` from `knitr` is the simplest route to a formatted table. `kableExtra` adds styling on top.

```{r, eval=FALSE}
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)

# Basic kable — format is inherited from the document output type
# use format = "html" or "latex" to force one
tidy(mod_cox, exponentiate = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE) %>%
  kable(digits = 3, caption = "Cox model hazard ratios")

# kableExtra styling — HTML output
tidy(mod_cox, exponentiate = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE) %>%
  kable(digits = 3, caption = "Cox model hazard ratios", format = "html") %>%
  kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
    full_width        = FALSE,
    position          = "left"
  ) %>%
  row_spec(0, bold = TRUE) %>%      # bold header row
  column_spec(1, bold = TRUE)       # bold first column

# Save an HTML table directly to file
tidy(mod_cox, exponentiate = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE) %>%
  kable(format = "html", digits = 3) %>%
  kable_styling() %>%
  save_kable("cox_table.html")

# Save as a self-contained image (.png) — requires webshot2
tidy(mod_cox, exponentiate = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE) %>%
  kable(format = "html", digits = 3) %>%
  kable_styling() %>%
  save_kable("cox_table.png")
```

Key `kable()` arguments:

| Argument    | What it does                                         |
|-------------|------------------------------------------------------|
| `digits`    | Decimal places for numeric columns                   |
| `caption`   | Table caption                                        |
| `col.names` | Override column names with a character vector        |
| `align`     | Column alignment: `"l"`, `"c"`, `"r"` or a vector    |
| `format`    | `"html"`, `"latex"`, `"pipe"` (markdown), `"simple"` |
| `escape`    | `FALSE` to allow raw HTML/LaTeX in cells             |

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Figures — `ggsave()`

`ggsave()` saves the most recently printed ggplot by default, or any plot object passed explicitly. Always pass the plot object explicitly to avoid saving the wrong plot.

```{r, eval=FALSE}
# Build and assign the plot first
p <- ggplot(lung, aes(x = age, y = time, color = sex)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  theme_minimal()

# PNG — standard raster format for slides and web
ggsave(
  filename = "lung_scatter.png",
  plot     = p,
  width    = 8,
  height   = 5,
  units    = "in",
  dpi      = 300        # 300 dpi is standard for publication
)

# PDF — vector format, scales without pixelation; good for journals
ggsave("lung_scatter.pdf", plot = p, width = 8, height = 5)

# SVG — vector format for web or further editing in Illustrator/Inkscape
ggsave("lung_scatter.svg", plot = p, width = 8, height = 5)

# Adjust base font size for publication sizing
p_pub <- p + theme_minimal(base_size = 11)
ggsave("lung_scatter_pub.png", plot = p_pub,
       width = 6.5, height = 4, dpi = 300)
```

Key `ggsave()` arguments:

| Argument | What it does |
|-------------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| `filename` | Output path; format inferred from file extension |
| `plot` | The ggplot object to save; defaults to last printed |
| `width`, `height` | Dimensions in `units` |
| `units` | `"in"` (default), `"cm"`, `"mm"`, `"px"` |
| `dpi` | Resolution; 300 for print, 150 for screen/web |
| `device` | Override format: `"png"`, `"pdf"`, `"svg"`, `"tiff"`, `"eps"` |
| `bg` | Background color; use `"white"` if theme has a transparent background |
------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Part 10: Quick Reference Card

## Package Lookup

| Package | Category | Key functions | Install note |
|-----------------|-----------------|--------------------|-------------------|
| `MASS` | GLM extensions | `polr()`, `glm.nb()`, `stepAIC()` | Base-adjacent; usually installed |
| `nnet` | Multinomial | `multinom()` | Base-adjacent |
| `VGAM` | Wide GLM family | `vglm()`, `vgam()` | Many family objects |
| `geepack` | GEE | `geeglm()` | Marginal models |
 Count data |
| `ordinal` | Ordinal models | `clm()`, `clmm()` | Better than `polr` for testing |
| `betareg` | Beta regression | `betareg()` | Proportions in (0,1) |
| `lmtest` | Testing | `lrtest()`, `waldtest()`, `coeftest()` | Any model class |
| `car` | Testing | `Anova()`, `vif()`, `leveneTest()` | Type II/III ANOVA |
| `performance` | Fit metrics | `check_model()`, `compare_performance()`, `r2()` | Works across model types |
| `DescTools` | Pseudo-R² | `PseudoR2()` | All variants |
| `pROC` | ROC curves | `roc()`, `auc()`, `ggroc()`, `coords()` |  |
| `survival` | Survival | `Surv()`, `survfit()`, `coxph()`, `cox.zph()` | Core survival package |
| `ggsurvfit` | KM plots | `ggsurvfit()`, `add_risktable()` | ggplot2 native |
| `survminer` | Survival plots | `ggsurvplot()`, `ggcoxzph()`, `ggforest()` | Alternative to ggsurvfit |
| `finalfit` | Tables + plots | `finalfit()`, `or_plot()`, `hr_plot()`, `summary_factorlist()` | Clinical workflow |
| `forestplot` | Forest plots | `forestplot()` | Full manual control |
| `gtsummary` | Pub tables | `tbl_summary()`, `tbl_regression()`, `tbl_uvregression()` | Excellent export |
| `MatchIt` | PS matching | `matchit()`, `match.data()` |  |
| `WeightIt` | IPW | `weightit()` | ATE/ATT weights |
| `cobalt` | PS balance | `love.plot()`, `bal.tab()` | Works with MatchIt + WeightIt |
| `factoextra` | PCA vis | `fviz_pca_biplot()`, `fviz_eig()` | Wraps `prcomp()` |
| `ggcorrplot` | Correlation | `ggcorrplot()` | ggplot2 native |
| `pheatmap` | Heatmaps | `pheatmap()` | Annotated heatmaps |
| `ggridges` | Distributions | `geom_density_ridges()` | Many groups |
| `ggrepel` | Labels | `geom_text_repel()`, `geom_label_repel()` | Avoid overlaps |
| `forecast` | Time series | `auto.arima()`, `forecast()`, `autoplot()` |  |
| `tsibble` | Time series | `as_tsibble()` | Modern TS data structure |
| `fable` | Time series | `ARIMA()`, `ETS()` | Tidy forecasting |

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Formula Syntax Cheatsheet

| Function | Package | Formula example | Notes |
|----------------|----------------|-------------------------|----------------|
| `lm()` | Base R | `y ~ x1 + x2` | Linear regression |
| `glm()` | Base R | `y ~ x1 + x2, family=binomial` | GLM; add `family` |
| `polr()` | `MASS` | `ordered_y ~ x1 + x2, Hess=TRUE` | Ordinal PO model |
| `clm()` | `ordinal` | `ordered_y ~ x1 + x2` | Cumulative link |
| `multinom()` | `nnet` | `factor_y ~ x1 + x2, trace=FALSE` | Multinomial |
| `vglm()` | `VGAM` | `y ~ x1 + x2, family=multinomial` | VGAM engine |
| `geeglm()` | `geepack` | `y ~ x1, id=id, corstr="exchangeable"` | GEE; needs `id` |
| `lmer()` | `lme4` | `y ~ x + (1 | group)` | Random intercepts |
| `glmer()` | `lme4` | `y ~ x + (1 | group), family=binomial` | GLMM |
| `coxph()` | `survival` | `Surv(time, status) ~ x1 + x2` | Cox PH |
| `survfit()` | `survival` | `Surv(time, status) ~ group` | KM estimator |
| `matchit()` | `MatchIt` | `treat ~ x1 + x2, method="nearest"` | PS matching |
| `weightit()` | `WeightIt` | `treat ~ x1 + x2, estimand="ATE"` | IPW |

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Model Selection Quick Guide

| Response type | Distribution | Start here | If problems → |
|------------------|-----------------|-----------------|--------------------|
| Continuous, normal errors | Gaussian | `lm()` | Transform response or use `Gamma` |
| Binary (0/1) | Binomial | `glm(..., family=binomial)` | Rare events → `cloglog`; separation → penalized via `glmnet` |
| Ordered categories | Multinomial/ordinal | `polr()` or `clm()` | PO violated → `vglm(parallel=FALSE)` |
| Unordered categories | Multinomial | `multinom()` | Many categories → `vglm()` |
| Counts, equidispersed | Poisson | `glm(..., family=poisson)` | Overdispersion → `glm.nb()` |
| Counts, overdispersed | Negative binomial | `glm.nb()` 
| Correlated binary | Binomial + clustering | `glmer(..., family=binomial)` or `geeglm()` | Many clusters → GEE; few → GLMM |
| Time-to-event | Survival | `coxph()` | PH violated → stratify or `tt()` |
